stem: A hospital uses a tTG-IgA antibody test to screen for celiac disease. The test has a sensitivity of 87% and a specificity of 82%. The prevalence of celiac disease in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.6% and 3.4%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has celiac disease? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(celiac disease | positive test) ranges from 0.0729 to 0.145 as prevalence varies from 1.6% to 3.4%. Point estimate at 2.5% prevalence: 0.11.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.18.
The confidence interval [0.0729, 0.145] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (87%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
